title: Message Passing Syntax
question: How do you call a method (send a message) in Objective-C, and how does it differ from a normal function call?
answer: Objective-C uses message passing with square-bracket syntax: [receiver message]. Instead of calling a function directly, you send a message to an object (the receiver), and the runtime looks up the correct method implementation at runtime. Arguments are supplied via named parameter labels, e.g. [array insertObject:obj atIndex:0].
explanation: The compiler translates [receiver selector:arg] into a call to objc_msgSend(receiver, @selector(selector:), arg). Because dispatch happens through the runtime, the method to execute is resolved dynamically based on the receiver's class, which is what enables features like categories, swizzling, and duck-typed id calls.
bestPractices: Read multi-part selectors as a whole, e.g. insertObject:atIndex:
Prefer dot syntax only for properties, brackets for methods
commonMistakes: Confusing method labels with separate arguments
Thinking messaging is the same as a static C function call
interviewTips: Mention objc_msgSend as the underlying dispatch function

title: The id Type
question: What is the id type in Objective-C and when would you use it?
answer: id is a generic pointer to any Objective-C object. It represents an object of unknown class and can receive any message. It is commonly used for polymorphism, collections that hold mixed types, and delegate references where the concrete type is not important.
explanation: id is defined as a pointer to an objc_object struct. Because it is untyped, the compiler does not perform static type checking on messages sent to an id, deferring resolution to runtime. This flexibility is powerful but sacrifices compile-time safety, so modern code often prefers concrete types or lightweight generics.
bestPractices: Use isKindOfClass: before assuming a concrete type
Prefer specific types when the class is known
commonMistakes: Writing id * (id is already a pointer)
Assuming id gives compile-time safety
interviewTips: Contrast id with instancetype for initializers

title: Messaging nil
question: What happens when you send a message to nil in Objective-C?
answer: Sending a message to nil is safe and does nothing — it simply returns a zero value (nil, 0, NO, or a zeroed struct depending on the return type). This is different from many languages where calling a method on null throws a null-pointer exception.
explanation: objc_msgSend checks whether the receiver is nil and, if so, short-circuits and returns 0 in the appropriate register. This behavior enables patterns like optional-chaining-style calls without explicit nil checks, but it can also hide bugs when a value is unexpectedly nil and the no-op silently produces wrong results.
bestPractices: Rely on nil-messaging deliberately, document the intent
Guard against unintended nil that masks logic errors
commonMistakes: Assuming a crash like Java NullPointerException
Relying on nil-messaging for struct returns without knowing edge cases
interviewTips: Note that struct return of a nil receiver is only guaranteed zeroed on modern runtimes

title: Retain, Release, Autorelease
question: Explain the manual reference counting methods retain, release, and autorelease.
answer: Under Manual Retain Release (MRR), retain increments an object's reference count, release decrements it, and autorelease schedules a later release when the current autorelease pool drains. When the count reaches zero, the object is deallocated. These form the basis of pre-ARC memory management.
explanation: Objective-C objects are heap-allocated and reference counted. You own any object you create with alloc/new/copy or that you explicitly retain, and you must balance ownership with a release or autorelease. autorelease is used to return objects you created without transferring ownership burden to the caller, deferring the release to when the pool drains (typically at the end of the run loop iteration).
bestPractices: Balance every retain/alloc/copy with a release
Return autoreleased objects from convenience methods
commonMistakes: Over-releasing and causing crashes
Forgetting to release, causing leaks
interviewTips: Mention that ARC automates these calls at compile time

title: Automatic Reference Counting (ARC)
question: What is ARC and how does it change memory management in Objective-C?
answer: Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) is a compiler feature that automatically inserts retain, release, and autorelease calls at compile time. Under ARC you no longer write these manually; instead you describe ownership with property qualifiers like strong and weak, and the compiler manages the reference counts for you.
explanation: ARC is not garbage collection — there is no runtime collector. The compiler analyzes object lifetimes and inserts the correct memory-management calls based on ownership semantics. This eliminates most leaks and over-releases, but developers must still avoid retain cycles, which ARC cannot break automatically.
bestPractices: Use weak for back-references to break cycles
Let ARC manage counts; never call retain/release under ARC
commonMistakes: Thinking ARC is garbage collection
Assuming ARC breaks retain cycles automatically
interviewTips: Stress ARC works at compile time, not runtime

title: Property Ownership Qualifiers
question: Compare strong, weak, assign, and copy for Objective-C properties.
answer: strong keeps a strong reference and increments the retain count (the default for object properties under ARC). weak holds a non-owning reference that is auto-set to nil when the target is deallocated. assign performs a simple pointer/value copy without ownership (used for primitives). copy stores an independent copy of the object, protecting against mutation.
explanation: Choosing the right qualifier defines ownership and lifetime. weak is essential for delegates and back-pointers to avoid retain cycles. copy is important for mutable classes like NSMutableString/NSMutableArray passed as a value type, since strong would retain the mutable original and let it change underneath you. assign is used for scalars (NSInteger, BOOL) or for non-zeroing unsafe references (unsafe_unretained).
bestPractices: Use copy for NSString and block properties
Use weak for delegates to break cycles
commonMistakes: Using strong for delegates and causing retain cycles
Using assign for objects instead of weak
interviewTips: Explain why NSString properties are typically copy

title: Retain Cycles
question: What is a retain cycle and how do you break it?
answer: A retain cycle occurs when two or more objects hold strong references to each other, so their reference counts never reach zero and they are never deallocated, causing a memory leak. You break cycles by making one of the references weak (or unowned), commonly on delegates and in block captures.
explanation: Because ARC uses reference counting rather than a tracing collector, mutually strong references form an island of objects that keep each other alive. The classic fix is a weak reference on the back-edge. Blocks are a frequent source: a block captured by an object that also captures self strongly creates a cycle, solved with a weak-self / strong-self dance.
bestPractices: Use weakSelf/strongSelf in blocks that capture self
Mark delegate properties as weak
commonMistakes: Strongly capturing self in a stored block
Forgetting weak on parent-child back references
interviewTips: Use Instruments' Leaks/Allocations to detect cycles

title: Categories
question: What are categories in Objective-C and what are their limitations?
answer: A category adds methods to an existing class without subclassing or having its source code — even to framework classes like NSString. It is declared with @interface ClassName (CategoryName). Categories are great for organizing code and extending built-in classes, but they cannot add instance variables and can override existing methods unpredictably.
explanation: Categories are resolved at runtime and their methods are merged into the class's method list. Because you cannot add ivars, you use associated objects (objc_setAssociatedObject) to attach state. If a category redefines an existing method, behavior becomes undefined when multiple categories collide, so overriding via categories is discouraged.
bestPractices: Prefix category method names to avoid collisions
Use associated objects when you need stored state
commonMistakes: Overriding existing methods via categories
Expecting to add ivars directly in a category
interviewTips: Contrast categories with class extensions (anonymous categories)

title: Protocols
question: What is a protocol in Objective-C and how do required vs optional methods work?
answer: A protocol declares a list of methods (and properties) that a conforming class can implement, similar to an interface in other languages. Methods are @required by default but can be marked @optional. A class adopts a protocol in angle brackets and must implement all required methods.
explanation: Protocols define contracts independent of class hierarchy, enabling polymorphism and decoupling — most notably in the delegation pattern. Because optional methods may not be implemented, callers should check with respondsToSelector: before invoking them. Protocols can also inherit from other protocols and be used to type variables like id<MyProtocol>.
bestPractices: Guard optional methods with respondsToSelector:
Have protocols inherit from NSObject protocol
commonMistakes: Calling optional methods without checking
Forgetting to declare conformance in the interface
interviewTips: Link protocols to the delegate pattern

title: Delegation Pattern
question: How does the delegation pattern work in Objective-C and why is the delegate weak?
answer: Delegation lets one object hand off responsibility for behavior or notifications to another object (the delegate) that conforms to a protocol. The delegating object holds a reference to its delegate and calls protocol methods on it at appropriate times. The delegate property is typically weak to avoid a retain cycle between the two objects.
explanation: Delegation is central to Cocoa (e.g. UITableViewDelegate). The pattern combines a protocol (the contract) with a weak delegate property (the connection). Because the delegate usually owns the delegating object (e.g. a view controller owns its table view), a strong delegate reference would create a cycle; weak breaks it and safely becomes nil if the delegate is deallocated.
bestPractices: Declare delegate properties as weak
Name delegate methods with the sender as first parameter
commonMistakes: Using strong delegates and leaking memory
Not checking respondsToSelector: for optional callbacks
interviewTips: Cite UITableView delegate/dataSource as examples

title: Blocks and __block
question: What are blocks in Objective-C and what does the __block qualifier do?
answer: Blocks are Objective-C's closures: self-contained units of code that capture variables from their enclosing scope. By default captured local variables are copied and read-only inside the block. Marking a variable with __block makes it mutable and shared between the block and the enclosing scope, so changes are visible on both sides.
explanation: When a block captures a non-__block local variable, it captures the value at creation time. __block changes the storage so the variable lives in shared memory the block references, allowing mutation. Blocks that are copied to the heap retain captured objects, which is why capturing self can create retain cycles — hence the weakSelf pattern.
bestPractices: Use __block only when you need mutation
Copy block properties to move them to the heap
commonMistakes: Assuming captured variables are mutable without __block
Creating retain cycles by capturing self strongly
interviewTips: Mention that block properties use copy, not strong

title: Selectors and @selector
question: What is a selector and how is @selector used in Objective-C?
answer: A selector (SEL) is the name of a method used to identify it at runtime — essentially a compiler-optimized string. @selector(name:) produces a SEL you can store, pass around, and use with the runtime, for example with performSelector:, respondsToSelector:, or target-action patterns.
explanation: Because dispatch is dynamic, methods are addressed by selector, not by pointer. SELs are registered in a global table so equal selector names map to the same SEL. This underpins introspection (respondsToSelector:), the target-action mechanism used by UI controls, and dynamic invocation via NSInvocation or performSelector:.
bestPractices: Match selector signatures exactly (colons matter)
Check respondsToSelector: before performSelector:
commonMistakes: Omitting the trailing colon for methods with arguments
Ignoring ARC warnings on performSelector: leaks
interviewTips: Explain SEL is a name, not a function pointer

title: Method Swizzling
question: What is method swizzling and what are its risks?
answer: Method swizzling is a runtime technique that swaps the implementations (IMPs) of two selectors, letting you replace or augment a method — even in framework classes — without subclassing. It is done via the Objective-C runtime with method_exchangeImplementations, typically in +load or a dispatch_once block.
explanation: Because Objective-C resolves methods through a per-class method list, you can exchange the IMP a selector points to at runtime. Swizzling is powerful for analytics, logging, or patching bugs, but it is fragile: it affects every instance globally, can break with framework updates, causes hard-to-debug behavior, and multiple swizzles can conflict. Use it sparingly and defensively.
bestPractices: Swizzle once inside dispatch_once
Call the original implementation to preserve behavior
commonMistakes: Swizzling outside +load/dispatch_once causing double swaps
Forgetting to call through to the original IMP
interviewTips: Note the global, app-wide impact of swizzling

title: Mutable vs Immutable Collections
question: Explain the difference between NSString/NSArray/NSDictionary and their mutable counterparts.
answer: Foundation provides immutable classes (NSString, NSArray, NSDictionary) and mutable subclasses (NSMutableString, NSMutableArray, NSMutableDictionary). Immutable objects cannot change after creation, making them thread-safer and cacheable. Mutable versions add methods to insert, remove, and modify contents in place.
explanation: The class-cluster design lets you pass immutable references safely because callers cannot mutate them. When exposing collection properties, use copy so that passing an NSMutableArray stores an immutable snapshot rather than retaining the caller's mutable object. Convert between them with mutableCopy and copy.
bestPractices: Expose collection properties as copy
Prefer immutable types unless mutation is required
commonMistakes: Mutating an object assigned to a strong NSArray property
Confusing copy (immutable) with mutableCopy
interviewTips: Mention thread-safety benefits of immutability

title: Key-Value Coding and Observing
question: What are KVC and KVO in Objective-C?
answer: Key-Value Coding (KVC) lets you access an object's properties indirectly by string key using valueForKey: and setValue:forKey:. Key-Value Observing (KVO) builds on KVC to notify observers automatically when an observed property changes, using addObserver:forKeyPath:options:context:.
explanation: KVC works by reflecting on accessor methods and ivars named after the key. KVO dynamically subclasses the observed object at runtime (isa-swizzling) to intercept setters and post change notifications. Properties must be KVO-compliant (use accessors or willChangeValueForKey:/didChangeValueForKey:). Observers must be removed before deallocation to avoid crashes.
bestPractices: Always remove observers in dealloc
Use a unique context pointer to disambiguate notifications
commonMistakes: Forgetting to remove observers, causing crashes
Observing non-KVO-compliant properties
interviewTips: Explain KVO uses isa-swizzling under the hood

title: @property and @synthesize
question: What do @property and @synthesize do, and what are property attributes?
answer: @property declares accessor methods (getter and setter) and an ivar for a value. @synthesize generates the accessor implementations and backing ivar (with modern compilers this is automatic, defaulting to an underscore-prefixed ivar). Property attributes like nonatomic, strong, copy, readonly, and getter=/setter= configure how the accessors behave.
explanation: Before automatic synthesis you wrote @synthesize name = _name; to create the ivar and accessors. Modern LLVM auto-synthesizes so you rarely write @synthesize unless you provide both custom accessors or need a custom ivar name. Attributes control atomicity, memory semantics, and mutability of the generated methods.
bestPractices: Rely on auto-synthesis; add @synthesize only when needed
Use getter=isX for BOOL properties
commonMistakes: Accessing _ivar before understanding auto-synthesis
Providing both accessors without @synthesize when an ivar is needed
interviewTips: Explain nonatomic vs atomic performance trade-off

title: Designated Initializers
question: What is a designated initializer and how does it relate to convenience initializers?
answer: A designated initializer is the primary init method that fully initializes an object, sets all its ivars, and calls its superclass's designated initializer. Convenience initializers are secondary inits that call a designated initializer (via self) rather than super, delegating the real work. This rule ensures a consistent, reliable initialization chain.
explanation: Every class has one (or few) designated initializers marked with NS_DESIGNATED_INITIALIZER. Subclasses must override the superclass designated initializer and route through their own. Convenience initializers keep call within the same class using self, while designated initializers reach upward with super, guaranteeing the whole hierarchy is initialized exactly once.
bestPractices: Convenience inits call self; designated inits call super
Mark the primary init with NS_DESIGNATED_INITIALIZER
commonMistakes: Calling super from a convenience initializer
Leaving ivars uninitialized in the designated init
interviewTips: Use instancetype as the return type for initializers

title: Fast Enumeration
question: What is fast enumeration in Objective-C and when should you use it?
answer: Fast enumeration is the for (Type item in collection) syntax that efficiently iterates over collections conforming to NSFastEnumeration (NSArray, NSSet, NSDictionary keys, etc.). It is faster and cleaner than index-based loops and prevents mutation of the collection during iteration.
explanation: Fast enumeration uses the countByEnumeratingWithState:objects:count: protocol method to fetch batches of items with minimal messaging overhead. Mutating the collection while enumerating throws an exception (mutation during enumeration). For index or concurrent access, use enumerateObjectsUsingBlock: which also provides the index and a stop flag.
bestPractices: Prefer fast enumeration for read-only iteration
Use enumerateObjectsUsingBlock: when you need the index
commonMistakes: Mutating a collection during fast enumeration
Using C-style index loops unnecessarily
interviewTips: Mention NSFastEnumeration as the enabling protocol

title: Format Specifiers
question: What format specifiers does NSLog / NSString use, and why is %@ special?
answer: Objective-C uses printf-style format specifiers plus %@ for objects. %@ calls an object's description method to print it. Common specifiers include %d/%ld for integers, %f for floats, %lu for NSUInteger (with an unsigned long cast), %p for pointers, and %@ for any object like NSString or NSNumber.
explanation: Because NSInteger/NSUInteger change width between 32-bit and 64-bit platforms, you typically cast to long/unsigned long and use %ld/%lu to silence warnings and stay portable. %@ is unique to Objective-C: it sends description (or debugDescription) to the object, which is why you can log arrays, dictionaries, and custom classes directly.
bestPractices: Cast NSInteger to long and use %ld
Use %@ to log objects via description
commonMistakes: Using %d for NSInteger on 64-bit platforms
Passing a C string where %@ expects an object
interviewTips: Explain %@ invokes the description method

title: Objective-C and Swift Interoperability
question: How do Objective-C and Swift interoperate in the same project?
answer: Objective-C and Swift can coexist via bridging. Swift sees Objective-C code through a bridging header (for app targets) or an umbrella header (for frameworks). Objective-C sees Swift classes through the auto-generated ProductName-Swift.h header, but only for classes that inherit from NSObject or are marked @objc.
explanation: The compiler generates interfaces automatically. To expose Swift APIs to Objective-C, mark them @objc (or subclass NSObject); Swift-only features like structs, enums with associated values, and generics are not visible. Conversely, most Objective-C APIs are imported into Swift with nullability annotations (nullable/nonnull) and lightweight generics improving the mapping. Nullability audits make bridged APIs cleaner in Swift.
bestPractices: Annotate Objective-C headers with nullability for cleaner Swift
Mark Swift APIs @objc to expose them to Objective-C
commonMistakes: Expecting Swift structs/enums to be visible in Objective-C
Forgetting to import the generated -Swift.h header
interviewTips: Mention the bridging header vs the generated -Swift.h header
